planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun (minimum 6 hours direct sun); Natchez is a large-growing crape myrtle — allow 20 feet of space in all directions at planting. Never top or 'crepe murder' — this destroys the tree's natural form permanently.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Natchez is widely considered the finest crape myrtle cultivar ever developed and is certainly the most widely planted — a National Arboretum hybrid of Lagerstroemia indica × L. fauriei introduced in 1987 that combines outstanding white flowers, four-season ornamental value, and exceptional disease resistance. From June through September in zone 9, Natchez produces massive clusters of pure white flowers continuously, creating a spectacular seasonal display that no other white-flowering summer tree rivals in the Deep South. In fall, the foliage transitions to brilliant orange-red — remarkable fall color for a Gulf Coast tree. But Natchez's most distinctive and lasting ornamental attribute may be its bark: the outer gray bark exfoliates in autumn to reveal rich cinnamon-brown, reddish-brown, and cream inner bark in a spectacular mottled pattern that makes the multi-trunked trunk one of the most beautiful in the tree world. The exfoliating bark is excellent as a winter feature when the tree is leafless. Natchez has outstanding resistance to powdery mildew — the primary disease problem of other crape myrtles. NEVER top (cut back major limbs) Natchez or any crape myrtle — this practice creates permanent, ugly knobby heads, destroys the tree's graceful form, and dramatically reduces its ornamental value without providing any benefit.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — National Arboretum Crape Myrtle Program

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Muskogee is one of the largest crape myrtle cultivars — allow 20–25 feet of clear space at planting to accommodate its mature size.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Muskogee is one of the most widely planted large crape myrtle cultivars, renowned for its soft, light lavender flower color — a cool, pale purple-pink that stands apart from the dominant pink and red tones of many crape myrtles and creates a particularly refreshing effect in the intense summer heat of the Gulf South. The flowers are produced in large clusters continuously from June through September in zone 9, covering the canopy with light lavender-purple throughout the peak summer season. Like Natchez (which it was developed alongside at the National Arboretum), Muskogee shows exceptional resistance to powdery mildew and retains attractive multi-trunked form when properly trained. The bark exfoliates to reveal mottled tan, gray, and cinnamon tones in late summer and fall. Fall leaf color transitions to orange-red, providing a third season of ornamental interest. Muskogee is a large-growing tree that can exceed 20–30 feet in height in zone 9 over decades, making it a true tree-scale specimen appropriate only for large planting spaces. Do not top or severely prune — always cut back only to lateral branches of similar diameter when any pruning is needed.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — National Arboretum Crape Myrtle Program

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Dynamite grows to a medium-large tree and should be given 12–15 feet of space in all directions at planting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Dynamite is among the finest red-flowered crape myrtle cultivars and one of the most popular landscape crape myrtles in the Gulf South — producing vivid, true cherry-red to scarlet flower clusters from June through September on a medium-large tree reaching 15–20 feet. True red is one of the most difficult colors to achieve reliably in crape myrtles — many claimed reds prove to be pink, magenta, or dark rose under Gulf Coast summer conditions — but Dynamite holds consistently true cherry-red color throughout the bloom season without fading. It was introduced by Dr. Carl Whitcomb and is marketed with the name 'Dynamite' reflecting the explosive intensity of its flower color. The multi-trunked form develops attractive exfoliating bark with age. Dynamite is somewhat more compact than the National Arboretum large-tree series and reaches 15–20 feet — large enough to function as a specimen shade tree but manageable in typical residential landscapes with some annual guidance pruning to remove crossing branches and maintain open, arching structure. Never top or 'crepe murder' — proper pruning removes only small, dead, and crossing branches, preserving the natural vase-shaped form.

common_name: Sioux Crape Myrtle (Dark Pink Crape Myrtle)

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Sioux is a medium-sized crape myrtle reaching 15–20 feet — appropriate for most residential settings where the large National Arboretum series would be too massive.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Sioux is a medium-sized National Arboretum hybrid crape myrtle producing deep hot-pink to rose-pink flower clusters from June through September — a vibrant, saturated pink that is warmer and more vivid than the typical light pinks common in older crape myrtle cultivars. The flowers are produced abundantly on an upright-spreading, multi-trunked tree reaching 15–20 feet in zone 9, providing both specimen tree and shade-tree functions for medium-sized residential landscapes. Like other National Arboretum hybrid crape myrtles, Sioux shows good resistance to powdery mildew compared to older Lagerstroemia indica cultivars. The exfoliating bark develops attractively with age, revealing warm tan to cinnamon tones beneath the peeling gray outer bark. Fall leaf color is orange-red. Sioux fills the important mid-sized slot between compact dwarf selections (under 10 feet) and the very large tree-sized Natchez and Muskogee, making it appropriate for typical suburban residential lot sizes where large-tree crape myrtles would eventually overwhelm the space. Remove any suckers that emerge from the base; lightly prune to remove crossing branches and dead wood only — do not top.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — National Arboretum Crape Myrtle Program

common_name: Tonto Crape Myrtle (Red Semi-Dwarf Crape Myrtle)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter, spring, or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Tonto is a semi-dwarf selection reaching 8–12 feet, making it suitable for smaller landscapes and foundation plantings where full-size crape myrtles would be overwhelming.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Tonto is a compact, semi-dwarf National Arboretum crape myrtle cultivar reaching 8–12 feet — smaller than the large tree-form selections but larger than the truly dwarf compact types — producing rich fuchsia-red to magenta-red flower clusters from midsummer through fall. It occupies the important size category that bridges the gap between full-size landscape trees and dwarf shrub-form crape myrtles, making it appropriate for foundation plantings of large structures, medium-sized residential landscapes, and situations requiring a crape myrtle that provides substantial presence without becoming a full-scale tree. Tonto shows exceptional resistance to powdery mildew — one of the best in the National Arboretum series for disease resistance — and produces glossy, deep mahogany-red fall foliage color that is among the finest in any crape myrtle selection. The developing exfoliating bark provides winter interest. Tonto benefits from annual removal of small crossing and rubbing branches and removal of sucker growth from the base to maintain an attractive multi-stemmed form. The fuchsia-red flower color is particularly vivid against light-colored walls and combines boldly with contrasting white or silver companion plantings.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — National Arboretum Crape Myrtle Program
